spec_match.rationale: Demonstrates the TC39 proposal's headline motivating example: many user terms mapped through `RegExp.escape` and joined with `|`. The naive panel actually throws on `C++`/`(parens)` while the safe panel matches each term literally.

interactive.rationale: Two editable fields (terms list + haystack) drive live highlighted side-by-side rendering with match counts and pattern previews on every keystroke.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb promises `many user terms joined with | into one alternation. Watch the naive path explode on the first ( while the escaped path keeps going` — that is exactly the failure mode rendered in the naive panel.

edge_cases.rationale: Catches the parse-error explosion well, but never shows the in-between case where the naive regex parses but matches the wrong stuff (e.g. `.NET` matching `aNET`/`bNET`), and never demonstrates the leading-digit hex-escape behaviour.

references.rationale: `see also` includes the chromestatus link and the TC39 proposal motivation page.

design_system.rationale: Inline styles are entirely CSS variables, no raw hex, no button-color overrides — clean design system usage.

openQuestions.title: Add the silently-wrong case to the comparison
openQuestions.detail: The naive panel currently goes red because the regex fails to parse. Equally important is the case where the naive regex *does* parse but matches the wrong text: feed `.NET, a.b` and the unescaped `.` metacharacter still matches `aNET`/`aXb`. Annotate that explicitly with a `false positives` count so the reader sees the silent-corruption failure mode, not just the loud-crash one.

openQuestions.title: Show the leading-digit hex-escape
openQuestions.detail: If a user adds a numeric term like `100mph` the escaped output should start with `\x31` per spec. The current polyfill emits this but the demo never points it out. A small `inspect escape` toggle that surfaces the per-term escape output character-by-character would close the loop.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the empty-term branch
openQuestions.detail: `terms.filter(Boolean)` quietly drops empty terms before the join. Add a row that intentionally includes a trailing comma to show what would happen with an empty alternative — `(a||b)` matches the empty string everywhere — and why the filter is necessary.

summary: Best-in-class side-by-side of the proposal's flagship motivating example. The only meaningful gap is that it dramatises the loud-crash failure mode but skips the equally important silently-wrong-matches mode — adding a false-positives readout would make the demo a complete spec teach.
